revision.text:
[[en:Chiriku Hachimangu]][[ja:千栗八幡宮]][[simple:Chiriku Hachiman-gū]][[zh:千栗八幡宮]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saga Prefecture, Japan|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building | name =Chiriku Hachiman-gū
千栗八幡宮 | image = Haiden of Chiriku Hachiman Shrine 2.jpg | caption = Haiden of Chiriku Hachiman-gū {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240|qq=}} | map_type = Japan Saga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|19|53.1|N|130|28|43.4|E|region:JP-41_scale:20000|display=inline,title|qq=}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = =Chiriku Hachiman-gū | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = Mibu Harunari | established = 724 | date_destroyed = | location = Senguri, Shirakabe, Miyaki-cho, Miyaki-gun, Saga-ken | website = | architecture_style = | festival =March 15 | leadership = | footnotes = }} {{nihongo|'''Chiriku Hachiman Shrine'''|千栗八幡宮|Chiriku Hachiman-gū|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Shirakabe neighborhood of the town of {{ill|Miyaki, Saga |en|Miyaki, Saga |qid=Q1351707|lt= Miyaki|1=Miyaki, Saga}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|qid=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture|1=Saga Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is one of the shrines claiming to be the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Hizen Province|en|Hizen Province|qid=Q906926|lt=Hizen Province|1=Hizen Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on March 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese|qq=}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Chiriku Hachiman-gū is {{nihongo|'''[[Hachiman]]'''|八幡神||qq=}}, which in this location is an amalgamation of: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}'''|応神天皇||qq=}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|qid=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}}'''|仲哀天皇||qq=}} * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}'''|神功皇后||qq=}} Secondary ''kami'' include: * {{nihongo|'''Naniwa-no-miko'''|難波皇子||qq=}}, son of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] * {{nihongo|'''Uji no Wakiiratsuko'''|菟道稚郎子||qq=}}, son of Emperor Ōjin * {{nihongo|'''[[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]'''|住吉明神||qq=}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]'''|武内宿禰|1=Takenouchi no Sukune||qq=}} ==History== According to the ''Chinzei Yoryaku'' (鎮西要略), in 724, Mibu Harunari, then governor of Hizen Province, received an oracle from the Hachiman deity to built a shrine on the land where a thousand chestnuts were growing. It became a dependency of the [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachiman-gū]] in the [[:en:Johei|Johei]] era (931-938). According to records of the {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|qid=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu (government)}}, in 999 oil gushed out of the ground at the shrine and was presented to the Imperial Court. It has been recognized as the ichinomiya of Hizen Province since the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, however, the title is disputed by the [[Yodohime Shrine]], located in the city of [[:en:Saga, Saga|Saga]]. The Chiriku Hachiman-gū burned down in December 1228. It is an indication of the importance of the shrine that the Court dispatched {{ill|Konoe Kanetsune|en|Konoe Kanetsune|qid=Q3198715|lt=Konoe Kanetsune|1=Konoe Kanetsune}} and Tsuchimikado Sadamichi, two high-Ranking [[kuge|nobles]] in 1230 to investigate and discuss the reconstruction. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the shrine was the site of numerous battles and was destroyed several times, most notable in 1534 when it was razed by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|en|Ōuchi clan|qid=Q1348909|lt=Ōuchi clan|1=Ōuchi clan}}; it was not rebuilt until 1583 under the sponsorship of the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|en|Ryūzōji clan|qid=Q1440180|lt=Ryūzōji clan|1=Ryūzōji clan}}. It continued to be supported by the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|en|Nabeshima clan|qid=Q3101516|lt=Nabeshima clan|1=Nabeshima clan}}, the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Saga Domain|en|Saga Domain|qid=Q2975995|lt=Saga Domain|1=Saga Domain}} during the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]. In 1609, it was awarded a plaque by {{ill|Emperor Go-Yōzei|en|Emperor Go-Yōzei|qid=Q349440|lt=Emperor Go-Yōzei|1=Emperor Go-Yōzei}} recognizing its position as ''ichinomiya'' of Hizen Province. {{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese|qq=}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was designated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]|qq=}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. {{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese|qq=}} ===Okayu Matsuri-=== The main festival of the shrine held on March 15th is affectionately called {{nihongo|Okayu-san|粥祭|||qq=}} by the locals, and involves a type of fortune-telling that uses {{ill|Congee|en|Congee|qid=Q878624|lt=rice porridge|1=Congee}} to predict whether the harvest will be good or bad that year. On February 16, porridge is boiled with water from the Hara River in front of the shrine, divided into four pots representing the four provinces of {{ill|Chikuzen Province|en|Chikuzen Province|qid=Q1047664|lt=Chikuzen|1=Chikuzen Province}}, {{ill|Chikugo Province|en|Chikugo Province|qid=Q864159|lt=Chikugo|1=Chikugo Province}}, Hizen, and {{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|qid=Q259705|lt=HIgo|1=Higo Province}} and placed in the shrine. It is taken out on March 15, and the direction where mold is growing is the region that will have a good harvest that year. ==Gallery== 肥前鳥居 059.jpg|Ichi-no-Torii, (Miyaki Town Tangible Cultural Property) ChirikuHachimangū Honden.JPG|Honden Precincts of ChirikuHachimangū.JPG|Precincts Chiriku Hachimangū Engi-e2.jpg|Muromachi period map ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8|qq=}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category|Chiriku Hachimangū|qq=}} *[https://chirikuhachiman.sakura.ne.jp Official home page] *[https://www.miyakikankou.jp/main.php/53.htmll Miyaki Tourist Information home page] ==Notes== {{reflist|qq=}} {{Shinto shrine|qq=}} {{Beppyo Shrines|qq=}} {{Ichinomiya|qq=}} [[en:Chiriku Hachimangu]] {{Commons category|Chiriku Hachimangū}} [[ja:千栗八幡宮]] [[simple:Chiriku Hachiman-gū]] [[zh:千栗八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11405620}} [[Category:Buildings in Miyaki]] [[Category:History of Miyaki]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal designated tangible cultural properties in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Saga Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Hizen Province]]
